_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
procedure_name: Surgical Correction of Supravalvular AS repair (Category - III Congenital Heart Disease)
clinical_indications: Symptomatic patients with peak instantaneous gradient ≥64 mmHg and/or mean gradient ≥50 mmHg on echo-Doppler (Class I)
Patients with mean Doppler gradient <50 mmHg, if they have any of the following (Class I): Symptoms attributable to obstruction (exertional dyspnea, angina, and syncope)
Patients with mean Doppler gradient <50 mmHg, if they have any of the following (Class I): Left ventricular systolic dysfunction attributable to obstruction
Patients with mean Doppler gradient <50 mmHg, if they have any of the following (Class I): Severe left ventricular hypertrophy attributable to obstruction
Patients with mean Doppler gradient <50 mmHg, if they have any of the following (Class I): Evidence of myocardial ischemia due to coronary ostial involvement
Asymptomatic patients with mean Doppler gradient ≥50 mmHg may be considered for surgery when the surgical risk is low (Class IIb)

clinical_thresholds.note: for asymptomatic patients with low surgical risk

claim.label: Post procedure stills of ECHO with report

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes contain detailed history, signs & symptoms, and indication for procedure?
Echo/Doppler report suggestive of Supravalvular Aortic Stenosis?

cpd_claim.q: Post procedure still of ECHO shows repair of the defect?
Detailed Discharge Summary mentions date of follow-up?

common_queries: Echo/Doppler report is not suggestive of Supravalvular Aortic Stenosis
Clinical notes missing detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure
Post procedure ECHO still does not show repair of the defect
Detailed Discharge Summary does not mention date of follow-up
Indoor case papers not submitted at claim stage
Procedure / Operative notes not submitted at claim stage

clinical_key_pointers: Aortic stenosis (AS) is most often due to stenosis of the aortic valve (80%-85%), but can also be due to obstruction below the valve (subvalvular, 15%, mostly due to discrete membrane) or above the valve (supravalvular, least common).
AS is more common in males.
The aortic valve can be unicuspid or bicuspid in patients with valvular AS.
Patients with unicuspid aortic valve present commonly in neonatal period with critical stenosis, whereas patients with bicuspid valve present more commonly during childhood.
Supravalvular stenosis is often associated with Williams–Beuren syndrome.
All patients with AS must be advised to maintain good oro-dental hygiene.
